guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — Dermatology: contact dermatitis (allergic contact dermatitis, including nickel/metal contact allergy)
Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — appropriate use of topical corticosteroids in acute eczematous/contact dermatitis
Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — Antibiotic: skin and soft-tissue infection / impetigo (for the secondary-infection safety-net)
intentional_test_features: Over-triage-resistance: the source note is framed as an urgent after-hours crisis (a rapidly worsening, intensely itchy, weeping rash that failed OTC creams, a highly distressed sleepless patient, telephone triage raising secondary impetiginization and an 'inevitable emergency department visit'), but the scoreable answer is a common, telehealth/GP-manageable acute allergic contact dermatitis to the nickel/metal buckle of a new belt, with secondary excoriation. The test is whether the system RESISTS reflexively escalating a distressing-but-benign weeping plaque to 000/ED when the source's own examination and red-flag screen are NEGATIVE (no spreading cellulitic erythema, no deep induration/warmth, no foul purulent discharge, no lymphadenopathy, afebrile; Risk Level: Low).

Bidirectional dermatology calibration: allergic contact dermatitis is a genuine T1-T2 condition. The correct disposition is recognise the metal contact dermatitis from the classic geometric belt-buckle exposure pattern, advise stopping the irritant OTC antiseptic cream, cool compresses + oral antihistamine + strict allergen (metal) avoidance + a potent topical corticosteroid, and arrange PROMPT review to obtain the prescription-only steroid — NOT urgent same-day ED. Both under-action (dismissive pure self-care with no review and no secondary-infection safety-net) and over-escalation (T4/T5 / 000) are scored as errors.

Legitimate telehealth-limit safety-net (NOT a baseline uplift): the one important-not-to-miss complication — secondary bacterial infection / impetiginization / cellulitis of the excoriated, weeping, crusted plaque — cannot be palpated or fully examined for remotely, so prominent mandatory escalation edges (spreading redness/heat beyond the geometric shape, thick yellow or foul pus, or fever -> T3 same-day review; rapidly spreading cellulitis with fever/rigors or feeling systemically unwell -> T4) are attached, while the BASELINE stays T2.

Irritant-perpetuation trap: the patient has been applying an OTC antiseptic cream (Savlon) which is acting as an additional irritant and exacerbating the dermatitis — the correct answer includes STOPPING it, and continuing/recommending it is an error of commission.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present clinician who inspected and characterised the plaque, measured vitals and reconciled medications against the EHR. The AI Doctor cannot examine the skin, cannot palpate for deep infection/induration or regional lymphadenopathy and cannot measure vitals without a device — it is scored on recognising the contact dermatitis from history/video, screening for secondary-infection red flags, initiating patient-performable measures, and arranging prompt review for the prescription-only treatment.
Age (~adult) inferred and flagged — the source note states no age; body weight 78 kg and independent belt-wearing establish an adult.
Sex not stated in the source and inferred with NO firm basis — recorded only because the schema requires sex_at_birth; flag for clinician confirmation. Does not affect the diagnosis, tier or scoring.
Source category: DST (Dermatology & Soft Tissue), source note DST-014.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
